title: Adult Vaccination Schedule
oneLiner: A framework for ensuring eligible adult inpatients receive recommended vaccines to prevent serious infections and outbreaks.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Live vaccine given to severely immunocompromised patient (e.g., on high-dose steroids, post-transplant, active haematological malignancy)** → Disseminated BCG infection, vaccine-strain measles pneumonitis, zoster dissemination. The single finding: patient on >20mg prednisolone/day for >2 weeks (or equivalent) AND prescribed MMR/BCG/LAIV/Herpes Zoster (Zostavax, not Shingrix) without checking immunosuppression.
- **Asplenic/hyposplenic patient with fever + rigors** → Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) – typically pneumococcal, meningococcal, or Haemophilus influenzae type b. Do not delay antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav) while waiting for vaccine; but also document that they need PCV13/PPV23 + MenACWY + MenB + HiB vaccines before discharge.
- **Unvaccinated pregnant woman exposed to rubella** → Congenital rubella syndrome. The splitter: check rubella IgG and pregnancy test – if seronegative and pregnant, immediate post-exposure prophylaxis (human normal immunoglobulin) is needed, not MMR (contraindicated in pregnancy).
sections.Presentation:
- **Which patients need vaccination?** Not a disease presentation – this is a preventive opportunity recognition.
- Key scenarios on the ward:
    - Any patient with newly diagnosed chronic condition (diabetes, COPD, CKD, heart failure, immunosuppression)
    - Pre-splenectomy / post-splenectomy or planned chemotherapy/immunosuppression
    - Pregnant women (pertussis, flu, COVID-19)
    - Healthcare workers (Hep B, flu, COVID-19)
    - Age-based: all adults ≥65 yrs (flu, pneumococcal, shingles [Shingrix], COVID-19)
    - Travel-related: check a travel clinic, not ward
- **Exam findings:** None specific – but check splenectomy scar, vaccination card, patient recall, GP summary.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- *This is a vaccine selection, not a disease differential.*
- **Age ≥65:** Flu + 23-valent pneumococcal (PPV23) + Shingrix (zoster vaccine recombinant, adjuvanted) + COVID-19 booster (annually or as per policy). **Splitter:** If patient has no chronic disease but is ≥65 – give flu, PPV23, Shingrix, COVID-19. If <65 and chronic disease – give flu (annually) + pneumococcal (PCV13 if asplenia/CKD, else PPV23) per schedule.
- **Asplenia/hyposplenia:** must give PCV13 (13-valent pneumococcal) first, then PPV23 after 8 weeks, then MenACWY, MenB, HiB – also annual flu and COVID-19. **Splitter:** Fever + no spleen – treat as OPSI, but discharge must include vaccine plan.
- **Immunocompromised (solid organ transplant, high-dose steroids, chemotherapy, primary immune deficiency):** Avoid live vaccines. Give PCV13, then PPV23 after 8 weeks; flu (inactivated); COVID-19; consider Shingrix (non-live) if >50 yrs; no MMR or BCG. **Splitter:** Check if on immunosuppression – if on >20mg pred >2 weeks, delay live vaccines until off therapy for >3 months (for steroids) or >6 months (for chemo).
- **Pregnancy:** Tdap (pertussis) at 20-32 weeks – give with each pregnancy; flu (inactivated) at any stage; COVID-19 (preferred mRNA); no MMR, BCG, LAIV. **Splitter:** If no antenatal care, ask about vaccination history; if unsure, check serology for rubella.
- **Healthcare worker:** Hep B (series + anti-HBs check), flu annually, COVID-19, consider BCG (if no Mantoux/IGRA positive history). **Splitter:** If needle-stick injury and positive HBsAg source – check anti-HBs; if <10 IU/L, give HBIG + booster.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Check temperature, conscious level (if suspect OPSI). Ask about vaccination history – look in handheld record, GP summary.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT (if immunosuppression), CRP, blood cultures (if fever). **Never-miss test:** rubella IgG in women of childbearing age (to guide MMR eligibility). Check anti-CCP and serology for Hep B (if starting immunosuppression or healthcare worker). For live vaccines – HIV test (if risk factors) and T cell count/PI status in suspected immunodeficiency.
- **Imaging:** Only if suspect infection.
- **Special tests:** Splenic volume on CT/ultrasound if hyposplenism suspected (e.g., sickle cell, coeliac disease). Mantoux/IGRA before BCG. Anti-HBs level 4-8 weeks after Hep B booster.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** [check local policy for vaccine schedules – published by NICE/BNF/Green Book]. Use the "patient group direction" for each vaccine.
- **Never-miss test:** Pregnancy test before any live vaccine in women of childbearing age (MMR, LAIV, BCG, varicella, yellow fever). Also: check if patient is on immunosuppressants – request lymphocyte count if uncertain.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If fever + asplenia → blood cultures, co-amoxiclav IV (or ceftriaxone if penicillin allergic) within 1 hour. Do not delay antibiotics for vaccination. Write vaccine plan on discharge summary.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Identify all vaccines indicated for the patient's age, comorbidities, and immunisation history. Order via pharmacy (need patient group directions). Give inactivated vaccines on same day (different sites if multiple) – live vaccines need checking for contraindications. Document in GP record and patient handheld.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Complete missed doses. For asplenia: give PCV13 first, then PPV23 8 weeks later, then MenACWY + MenB + HiB. For CKD/transplant: annual flu, COVID-19, pneumococcal, consider Shingrix. For pregnancy: Tdap each pregnancy.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A – vaccination is not a treatment order. But ensure VTE prophylaxis and usual care continue.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Influenza vaccine (inactivated):** 0.5mL IM once annually. Contraindicated if severe egg allergy (use egg-free cell-based if available locally).
- **23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPV23):** 0.5mL IM once for ≥65 yrs, asplenia, CKD, immunocompromised, chronic lung/heart/liver. Revaccination every 5-10 years? [check local policy: usually one dose, some immunocompromised need repeat after 5 years].
- **13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13):** 0.5mL IM once first then PPV23 after 8 weeks in high-risk (asplenia, immunocompromised, CSF leak). Never give both PCV13 and PPV23 on same day.
- **MMR (live):** 0.5mL SC or IM – two doses at least 1 month apart. **Contraindications:** pregnancy, immunosuppression, severe anaphylaxis to neomycin/gelatin.
- **BCG (live):** 0.1mL intradermally (0.05mL for <12 months) – single dose. **Contraindications:** pregnancy, immunocompromised, previous BCG, positive Mantoux/IGRA. **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving BCG intradermally to a patient who has a positive IGRA (can cause severe local reaction – treat as TB infection).
- **Shingrix (recombinant zoster):** 0.5mL IM – two doses 2 months apart for ≥50 yrs and immunocompromised ≥18 yrs. **No serious dose error – safety profile good.**
- **MenACWY conjugate:** 0.5mL IM – one dose for asplenia, complement deficiency, travel to Hajj. Give at least 2 weeks before splenectomy.
- **MenB (4CMenB/Trumenba):** [check local policy – two doses 8 weeks apart for asplenia/complement deficiency] 0.5mL IM.
- **HiB:** 0.5mL IM – one dose for asplenia (if not given in childhood). Use ActHiB or other.
- **Hepatitis B vaccine (Engerix B or similar):** 20mcg IM (in deltoid) at 0, 1, 6 months. For rapid schedule (post-exposure) give at 0, 1, 2, 12 months. **Most dangerous dose error:** giving IM in gluteal region (reduces immunogenicity). Always deltoid. Check anti-HBs 4-8 weeks after completion.
- **Tdap (Boostrix/Adacel):** 0.5mL IM – single dose each pregnancy at 20-32 weeks. Also one dose for adults with no previous tetanus booster >10 years.
- **COVID-19 vaccines:** BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273 – primary courses + boosters according to age/risk. [Check local policy for current strain updates.]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustments needed for inactivated vaccines. Live vaccines generally contraindicated if advanced CKD (eGFR <15). Hepatic impairment no change.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving MMR to a patient on immunosuppression – can cause measles pneumonitis. Always screen.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Anaphylaxis (rare – have adrenaline 1:1000 available). Syncope (post-vaccination faint – common in adolescents). Injection site pain, fever, myalgia (especially flu, Shingrix). Local BCG reaction (ulceration, lymphadenitis – usually self-limiting).
- **Chronic:** BCG osteitis (rare). Vaccine-strain infection in immunocompromised (live vaccines). Varicella-like rash post-MMR (non-infectious). Guillain-Barré syndrome (very rare – flu, Shingrix). Myopericarditis post-mRNA COVID-19 (young males – rare, monitor chest pain for 1 week).
sections.Prognosis: - Vaccination dramatically reduces morbidity and mortality from these infections. Population-level effectiveness: influenza vaccine 40-60% (seasonal), pneumococcal 50-80% vs invasive disease (serotype dependent), Shingrix >90% in preventing shingles in >50 yrs. Given the multiple comorbid patients on the ward, ensuring up-to-date vaccination is a high-impact preventive intervention.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check vaccination status on admission** – ask "Are you up to date with jabs?" Look at GP summary or letter. Document plan on discharge.
- **Give multiple inactivated vaccines in different limbs on same day** – but separate live vaccines by at least 4 weeks if not given together (MMR and varicella can be given same day, but not MMR and BCG).
- **Order vaccines via pharmacy early** – many wards have a supply of flu, PPV23, and COVID-19. For specials (MenACWY, MenB, HiB, Shingrix) you may need to requisition.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving a live vaccine without checking pregnancy or immunosuppression** – fatal errors. Always do a pregnancy test and ask about steroids/chemo/transplant.
- **Delaying discharge to vaccinate** – if patient is going home, write a GP letter to arrange vaccination within 2 weeks; do not keep them in hospital just for a jab. But for asplenia, give PCV13 before discharge.
- **Assuming childhood vaccines are complete** – many adults missed MenB, rotavirus, varicella, HiB (not in old childhood schedule). Check if born before 2006 (schedule changes) and immunocompromised/asplenic – need catch-up.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Vacination site becomes red, hot, enlarging >3cm (especially BCG – could be abscess, require incision). Fever >39°C or lethargy within 48h post-vaccine. Any signs of anaphylaxis (urticaria, wheeze, hypotension) – seek immediate emergency care.
- **Follow-up:** GP appointment in 2-4 weeks to complete remaining vaccine series (e.g., second dose of Shingrix, MenB). For asplenia: ensure antibiotics (phenoxymethylpenicillin) prescribed and given, and that patient wears a medical alert bracelet.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:** You are unsure about contraindications to a live vaccine (immunosuppressed depth). Suspected anaphylaxis – give IM adrenaline 0.5mg (1:1000) and call crash team. Fever with rigors in an asplenic patient – start antibiotics immediately.
- **Call ICU / crash team:** Anaphylaxis, angioedema, respiratory distress. NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >4 from suspected vaccine-preventable infection.
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS <15, systolic BP <90, oxygen saturations <92% on air. In suspected meningococcal disease – call senior immediately; give IV ceftriaxone within 1 hour and consider transfer to ICU.

rawMarkdown:
# Adult Vaccination Schedule
**One-liner:** A framework for ensuring eligible adult inpatients receive recommended vaccines to prevent serious infections and outbreaks.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Live vaccine given to severely immunocompromised patient (e.g., on high-dose steroids, post-transplant, active haematological malignancy)** → Disseminated BCG infection, vaccine-strain measles pneumonitis, zoster dissemination. The single finding: patient on >20mg prednisolone/day for >2 weeks (or equivalent) AND prescribed MMR/BCG/LAIV/Herpes Zoster (Zostavax, not Shingrix) without checking immunosuppression.
- **Asplenic/hyposplenic patient with fever + rigors** → Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) – typically pneumococcal, meningococcal, or Haemophilus influenzae type b. Do not delay antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav) while waiting for vaccine; but also document that they need PCV13/PPV23 + MenACWY + MenB + HiB vaccines before discharge.
- **Unvaccinated pregnant woman exposed to rubella** → Congenital rubella syndrome. The splitter: check rubella IgG and pregnancy test – if seronegative and pregnant, immediate post-exposure prophylaxis (human normal immunoglobulin) is needed, not MMR (contraindicated in pregnancy).

### Presentation
- **Which patients need vaccination?** Not a disease presentation – this is a preventive opportunity recognition.
- Key scenarios on the ward:
    - Any patient with newly diagnosed chronic condition (diabetes, COPD, CKD, heart failure, immunosuppression)
    - Pre-splenectomy / post-splenectomy or planned chemotherapy/immunosuppression
    - Pregnant women (pertussis, flu, COVID-19)
    - Healthcare workers (Hep B, flu, COVID-19)
    - Age-based: all adults ≥65 yrs (flu, pneumococcal, shingles [Shingrix], COVID-19)
    - Travel-related: check a travel clinic, not ward
- **Exam findings:** None specific – but check splenectomy scar, vaccination card, patient recall, GP summary.

### Differential Diagnosis
- *This is a vaccine selection, not a disease differential.*
- **Age ≥65:** Flu + 23-valent pneumococcal (PPV23) + Shingrix (zoster vaccine recombinant, adjuvanted) + COVID-19 booster (annually or as per policy). **Splitter:** If patient has no chronic disease but is ≥65 – give flu, PPV23, Shingrix, COVID-19. If <65 and chronic disease – give flu (annually) + pneumococcal (PCV13 if asplenia/CKD, else PPV23) per schedule.
- **Asplenia/hyposplenia:** must give PCV13 (13-valent pneumococcal) first, then PPV23 after 8 weeks, then MenACWY, MenB, HiB – also annual flu and COVID-19. **Splitter:** Fever + no spleen – treat as OPSI, but discharge must include vaccine plan.
- **Immunocompromised (solid organ transplant, high-dose steroids, chemotherapy, primary immune deficiency):** Avoid live vaccines. Give PCV13, then PPV23 after 8 weeks; flu (inactivated); COVID-19; consider Shingrix (non-live) if >50 yrs; no MMR or BCG. **Splitter:** Check if on immunosuppression – if on >20mg pred >2 weeks, delay live vaccines until off therapy for >3 months (for steroids) or >6 months (for chemo).
- **Pregnancy:** Tdap (pertussis) at 20-32 weeks – give with each pregnancy; flu (inactivated) at any stage; COVID-19 (preferred mRNA); no MMR, BCG, LAIV. **Splitter:** If no antenatal care, ask about vaccination history; if unsure, check serology for rubella.
- **Healthcare worker:** Hep B (series + anti-HBs check), flu annually, COVID-19, consider BCG (if no Mantoux/IGRA positive history). **Splitter:** If needle-stick injury and positive HBsAg source – check anti-HBs; if <10 IU/L, give HBIG + booster.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Check temperature, conscious level (if suspect OPSI). Ask about vaccination history – look in handheld record, GP summary.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT (if immunosuppression), CRP, blood cultures (if fever). **Never-miss test:** rubella IgG in women of childbearing age (to guide MMR eligibility). Check anti-CCP and serology for Hep B (if starting immunosuppression or healthcare worker). For live vaccines – HIV test (if risk factors) and T cell count/PI status in suspected immunodeficiency.
- **Imaging:** Only if suspect infection.
- **Special tests:** Splenic volume on CT/ultrasound if hyposplenism suspected (e.g., sickle cell, coeliac disease). Mantoux/IGRA before BCG. Anti-HBs level 4-8 weeks after Hep B booster.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** [check local policy for vaccine schedules – published by NICE/BNF/Green Book]. Use the "patient group direction" for each vaccine.
- **Never-miss test:** Pregnancy test before any live vaccine in women of childbearing age (MMR, LAIV, BCG, varicella, yellow fever). Also: check if patient is on immunosuppressants – request lymphocyte count if uncertain.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If fever + asplenia → blood cultures, co-amoxiclav IV (or ceftriaxone if penicillin allergic) within 1 hour. Do not delay antibiotics for vaccination. Write vaccine plan on discharge summary.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Identify all vaccines indicated for the patient's age, comorbidities, and immunisation history. Order via pharmacy (need patient group directions). Give inactivated vaccines on same day (different sites if multiple) – live vaccines need checking for contraindications. Document in GP record and patient handheld.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Complete missed doses. For asplenia: give PCV13 first, then PPV23 8 weeks later, then MenACWY + MenB + HiB. For CKD/transplant: annual flu, COVID-19, pneumococcal, consider Shingrix. For pregnancy: Tdap each pregnancy.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A – vaccination is not a treatment order. But ensure VTE prophylaxis and usual care continue.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Influenza vaccine (inactivated):** 0.5mL IM once annually. Contraindicated if severe egg allergy (use egg-free cell-based if available locally).
- **23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPV23):** 0.5mL IM once for ≥65 yrs, asplenia, CKD, immunocompromised, chronic lung/heart/liver. Revaccination every 5-10 years? [check local policy: usually one dose, some immunocompromised need repeat after 5 years].
- **13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13):** 0.5mL IM once first then PPV23 after 8 weeks in high-risk (asplenia, immunocompromised, CSF leak). Never give both PCV13 and PPV23 on same day.
- **MMR (live):** 0.5mL SC or IM – two doses at least 1 month apart. **Contraindications:** pregnancy, immunosuppression, severe anaphylaxis to neomycin/gelatin.
- **BCG (live):** 0.1mL intradermally (0.05mL for <12 months) – single dose. **Contraindications:** pregnancy, immunocompromised, previous BCG, positive Mantoux/IGRA. **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving BCG intradermally to a patient who has a positive IGRA (can cause severe local reaction – treat as TB infection).
- **Shingrix (recombinant zoster):** 0.5mL IM – two doses 2 months apart for ≥50 yrs and immunocompromised ≥18 yrs. **No serious dose error – safety profile good.**
- **MenACWY conjugate:** 0.5mL IM – one dose for asplenia, complement deficiency, travel to Hajj. Give at least 2 weeks before splenectomy.
- **MenB (4CMenB/Trumenba):** [check local policy – two doses 8 weeks apart for asplenia/complement deficiency] 0.5mL IM.
- **HiB:** 0.5mL IM – one dose for asplenia (if not given in childhood). Use ActHiB or other.
- **Hepatitis B vaccine (Engerix B or similar):** 20mcg IM (in deltoid) at 0, 1, 6 months. For rapid schedule (post-exposure) give at 0, 1, 2, 12 months. **Most dangerous dose error:** giving IM in gluteal region (reduces immunogenicity). Always deltoid. Check anti-HBs 4-8 weeks after completion.
- **Tdap (Boostrix/Adacel):** 0.5mL IM – single dose each pregnancy at 20-32 weeks. Also one dose for adults with no previous tetanus booster >10 years.
- **COVID-19 vaccines:** BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273 – primary courses + boosters according to age/risk. [Check local policy for current strain updates.]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustments needed for inactivated vaccines. Live vaccines generally contraindicated if advanced CKD (eGFR <15). Hepatic impairment no change.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving MMR to a patient on immunosuppression – can cause measles pneumonitis. Always screen.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Anaphylaxis (rare – have adrenaline 1:1000 available). Syncope (post-vaccination faint – common in adolescents). Injection site pain, fever, myalgia (especially flu, Shingrix). Local BCG reaction (ulceration, lymphadenitis – usually self-limiting).
- **Chronic:** BCG osteitis (rare). Vaccine-strain infection in immunocompromised (live vaccines). Varicella-like rash post-MMR (non-infectious). Guillain-Barré syndrome (very rare – flu, Shingrix). Myopericarditis post-mRNA COVID-19 (young males – rare, monitor chest pain for 1 week).

### Prognosis
- Vaccination dramatically reduces morbidity and mortality from these infections. Population-level effectiveness: influenza vaccine 40-60% (seasonal), pneumococcal 50-80% vs invasive disease (serotype dependent), Shingrix >90% in preventing shingles in >50 yrs. Given the multiple comorbid patients on the ward, ensuring up-to-date vaccination is a high-impact preventive intervention.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check vaccination status on admission** – ask "Are you up to date with jabs?" Look at GP summary or letter. Document plan on discharge.
- **Give multiple inactivated vaccines in different limbs on same day** – but separate live vaccines by at least 4 weeks if not given together (MMR and varicella can be given same day, but not MMR and BCG).
- **Order vaccines via pharmacy early** – many wards have a supply of flu, PPV23, and COVID-19. For specials (MenACWY, MenB, HiB, Shingrix) you may need to requisition.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving a live vaccine without checking pregnancy or immunosuppression** – fatal errors. Always do a pregnancy test and ask about steroids/chemo/transplant.
- **Delaying discharge to vaccinate** – if patient is going home, write a GP letter to arrange vaccination within 2 weeks; do not keep them in hospital just for a jab. But for asplenia, give PCV13 before discharge.
- **Assuming childhood vaccines are complete** – many adults missed MenB, rotavirus, varicella, HiB (not in old childhood schedule). Check if born before 2006 (schedule changes) and immunocompromised/asplenic – need catch-up.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Vacination site becomes red, hot, enlarging >3cm (especially BCG – could be abscess, require incision). Fever >39°C or lethargy within 48h post-vaccine. Any signs of anaphylaxis (urticaria, wheeze, hypotension) – seek immediate emergency care.
- **Follow-up:** GP appointment in 2-4 weeks to complete remaining vaccine series (e.g., second dose of Shingrix, MenB). For asplenia: ensure antibiotics (phenoxymethylpenicillin) prescribed and given, and that patient wears a medical alert bracelet.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:** You are unsure about contraindications to a live vaccine (immunosuppressed depth). Suspected anaphylaxis – give IM adrenaline 0.5mg (1:1000) and call crash team. Fever with rigors in an asplenic patient – start antibiotics immediately.
- **Call ICU / crash team:** Anaphylaxis, angioedema, respiratory distress. NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >4 from suspected vaccine-preventable infection.
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS <15, systolic BP <90, oxygen saturations <92% on air. In suspected meningococcal disease – call senior immediately; give IV ceftriaxone within 1 hour and consider transfer to ICU.
